title: First-principles prediction of chiral-phonon-induced orbital accumulation
authors: A. Pezo, A. Manchon, Y. Nii, K. Ando, T. Kato
abstract: Chiral phonons offer a route to transfer angular momentum without relying on magnetic order, but their electronic response in metals remains poorly understood from perspectives beyond spin-based scenarios. Using first-principles calculations, we show that coherent chiral lattice motion generates orbital accumulation and, through spin-orbit coupling, a smaller accompanying spin accumulation. Our approach evaluates orbital and spin expectation values directly from strain perturbed ab initio Hamiltonians in the long-wavelength limit, where the phonon perturbation is represented by symmetry adapted circular lattice distortions. We show that the response is controlled mainly by orbital character, near-degeneracies, and electron-phonon coupling, rather than by spin-orbit coupling alone. These results identify light transition metals as promising platforms for chiral-phonon-driven orbitronics.
summary: This paper uses first-principles calculations to show that chiral phonons can generate significant orbital accumulation in metals, even in the absence of strong spin-orbit coupling. It identifies light transition metals as superior platforms for orbitronics due to their orbital character and near-degenerate electronic states. The study highlights that orbital-to-spin conversion is more efficient in heavier elements.
main_problem: Understanding the microscopic origins and material-dependent strength of orbital and spin accumulations induced by coherent chiral lattice motion in metals.
main_result: Orbital accumulation is much larger than spin accumulation and is driven by orbital character and near-degeneracy rather than spin-orbit coupling alone, with light transition metals being promising for orbitronics.
method: First-principles calculations using the SIESTA implementation to evaluate deformation operators via finite differences between strained ab initio Hamiltonians.
model_or_system: Non-magnetic transition metals (e.g., Ti, Nb, Mo, Ru, Pt, Ta, W) and Silicon subjected to long-wavelength, coherent transverse acoustic chiral phonons modeled as circular lattice distortions.
key_observables: Orbital accumulation (Lz) and spin accumulation (Sz), and the ratio of spin-to-orbital accumulation.
important_parameters: Long-wavelength, adiabatic limit; lattice displacement amplitude of 10^-3 Angstroms; spin-orbit coupling strength; orbital texture.
main_assumptions: Uses the adiabatic approximation and the long-wavelength limit.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows a schematic of chiral lattice excitation; Fig 2 compares orbital and spin accumulations across materials and shows the spin-to-orbital ratio; Fig 3 shows band structures projected onto orbital angular momentum; Fig 4 shows experimental detection setups.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of chiral phonon-induced electronic response, presents a first-principles framework for calculating orbital/spin accumulation, analyzes material-dependent trends (orbital vs. spin), discusses microscopic drivers like orbital texture and degeneracy, and concludes with experimental detection implications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the chiral lattice excitation considered in this work. (a) Chiral long-wavelength phonon propagating in a nonmagnetic target (NM) along the z di- rection. The lattice displacement is represented by circular motion. (b) Schematic of the induced strain ϵzx due to the chiral phonon.

FIG. 2. Orbital and spin accumulations induced by the adia- batic chiral strain cycle. (a) Orbital and spin accumulations for the materials considered in this work. Several light tran- sition metals exhibit larger orbital accumulation than Pt de- spite its stronger SOC. (b) Ratio ⟨ˆSz⟩/ ⟨ˆLz⟩, illustrating more efficient orbital-to-spin conversion in heavier metals.

FIG. 4. Schematic experimental setups for detecting orbital and spin accumulation induced by chiral acoustic phonon, us- ing a ferromagnetic insulator (FI) and a nonmagnetic insula- tor (NMI). (a) Injection of a propagating chiral phonon mode into a target. (b) Standing wave of a chiral phonon with the target placed on the nonmagnetic insulating spacer.

FIG. 3. Microscopic origin of the contrasting responses in Ti and Pt. Orbital texture projected onto the band structures of Ti (a) and Pt (c), together with the corresponding electron- phonon and orbital Berry-curvature indicators in Ti (b) and Pt (d). Ti exhibits stronger orbital texture, more favorable near-degeneracies, and a larger strain-induced response than Pt despite its weaker SOC.
